section.title: A New Heaven and a New Earth
section.sectionRationale: Revelation 21:1-8 opens the seventh and final vision cycle of the book. After the judgment of Babylon, the beast, the false prophet, and the devil in chapters 19-20, the scene turns at last to God's own dwelling with His people. This unit is a self-contained overture, a declaration straight from the throne, that stands before the angel's detailed, guided tour of the New Jerusalem begins in 21:9-22:5. It closes with a stark either/or, overcomers who inherit all things and the unrepentant who face the second death, marking a clean seam before the next section's extended vision of the bride city.
section.thesis: The dwelling of God with man, promised since Eden and pictured in the tabernacle, is here declared complete: the old order of death, mourning, and pain passes away, all things are made new, and every reader is confronted with the choice between overcoming faith and the second death.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the new heaven and new earth with the holy city descending like a bride; the voice from the throne announcing that God's dwelling is now with man; the enthroned One's declaration, 'Behold, I make all things new,' and His self-naming as the Alpha and the Omega; and the closing contrast between the one who overcomes and those who are shut out into the second death.

units.label: A new heaven and a new earth

verses.text: Then I saw a {{kainos|new}} heaven and a {{kainos|new}} earth, for the first heaven and earth had passed away, and the sea was no more.
I saw the holy city, the {{kainos|new}} Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband.

And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying: “Behold, the {{skenoo|dwelling place}} of God is with man, and He will {{skenoo|dwell}} with them. They will be His people, and God Himself will be with them as their God.
‘He will wipe away every tear from their eyes,’ and there will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain, for the former things have passed away.”

units.label: Behold, I make all things new

verses.text: And the One seated on the throne said, “Behold, I make all things {{kainos|new}}.” Then He said, “Write this down, for these words are faithful and true.”
And He told me, “It is done! I am the {{alpha_omega|Alpha and the Omega}}, the Beginning and the End. To the thirsty I will give freely from the spring of the water of life.
The one who {{nikao|overcomes}} will inherit all things, and I will be his God, and he will be My son.

But to the cowardly and unbelieving and abominable and murderers and sexually immoral and sorcerers and idolaters and all liars, their place will be in the lake that burns with fire and sulfur. This is the second death.”

kainos.gloss: New in kind or quality, as distinct from neos, new merely in time. The new heaven, new earth, and new Jerusalem are not simply young; they are a fresh, transformed order altogether. The same word governs the throne's own declaration, 'Behold, I make all things new.'

skenoo.gloss: To tabernacle or dwell, drawn from skene, a tent. It recalls the tabernacle where God dwelt among Israel behind a veil, Exodus 25:8, and the Word who 'tabernacled' among us, John 1:14. Here the dwelling is at last permanent, direct, and unveiled.

alpha_omega.gloss: The first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, a title of eternity and total sovereignty over history from its beginning to its end. God takes this title for Himself here, and the risen Christ takes the identical title in Revelation 1:8, 1:17, and 22:13.

nikao.gloss: To conquer or overcome. It is the repeated call to the seven churches in Revelation 2-3, and here it names the one who perseveres in faith in Christ rather than joining the list of verse 8. To overcome is not sinless perfection but persevering trust in the Lamb who has already conquered.

basic.context: After Babylon and the beast are judged, John sees the goal of the whole story: a new heaven and a new earth, and the holy city coming down like a bride. A voice from the throne announces that God will now live with His people directly, with no more death, tears, or pain.
basic.hermeneutics: Most readers take this as a real, future, physical hope, though they debate whether it is this creation renewed or an entirely fresh creation. Either way, the passage is a promise to be received in hope, and its one command to the reader is to overcome rather than to shrink back.
basic.originalLanguages: The word for 'new,' kainos, means new in quality, not just new in time. God will 'tabernacle,' skenoo, with His people, unveiled at last. He calls Himself the Alpha and the Omega, the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, and promises that 'the one who overcomes,' using the same Greek word, nikao, found throughout Revelation, will inherit all things.
basic.typology: This is the direct answer to Genesis 3, where God's presence withdrew and a flaming sword barred the way back to the tree of life. Here God Himself comes to dwell with man without any barrier at all, and the tabernacle's veiled presence among Israel becomes an unveiled, permanent dwelling.
basic.christ: The One who says 'It is done' and takes the title Alpha and Omega is Christ, who said 'It is finished' on the cross and takes this very title again in Revelation 22:13. His finished work secures both salvation and this new creation.
basic.god: God is the one who at last dwells with His people without distance, faithful and true to His own promises, sovereign to make all things new, and just, drawing a clear line between those who overcome and those who do not.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 65:17 promises new heavens and a new earth. Isaiah 25:8 promises God will wipe away every tear, quoted almost exactly here. Ezekiel 37:27 promises God's dwelling among His people. Genesis 3:24 is the exile this passage reverses.
basic.oneStory: Genesis 3 ended with humanity driven out and a flaming sword guarding the tree of life. Revelation 21 begins the answer: God Himself comes down to dwell with His people forever, wiping away the very tears that began in the garden, and making all things new.

context.literary: Opens the seventh and final vision cycle of Revelation, following the destruction of Babylon and the judgment of the beast, false prophet, and devil in chapters 19-20. It functions as a summary overture, declared straight from the throne, before the angel's guided tour of the New Jerusalem's details begins in 21:9-22:5.
context.historical: Written to churches under real pressure, some facing exile, imprisonment, and death for refusing to worship Rome, this vision answers present suffering with the certain hope of God's unmediated presence and the end of every grief that persecution brings.
context.canonical: The direct fulfillment of Isaiah's promised new heavens and new earth, Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22, and of the tabernacle and temple hope that God would dwell among His people, Exodus 25:8 and Ezekiel 37:27. It is also the reversal of Genesis 3, where God's presence withdraws and death enters, undone here in a single scene.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure suffering, exiled believers that history ends not in loss but in God Himself dwelling fully with His redeemed people in a renewed creation where death, mourning, and pain are undone forever.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a description of what God will certainly do, to be received in hope rather than obeyed as a command; its one imperative charge to the reader is implicit in verse 7, to overcome, holding fast to Christ rather than sliding into the list of verse 8.

debates.question: Does 'a new heaven and a new earth' mean this creation renewed, or the old one ended and replaced?

views.label: Renewal of this creation
views.case: Kainos means new in kind or quality, not new in time, neos; Romans 8:19-21 pictures creation itself set free from bondage to decay rather than destroyed, so many read this as the present creation gloriously renewed and purged, continuous with the world we know.

views.label: A wholly new act of creation
views.case: 2 Peter 3:10-13 speaks of the heavens passing away with a roar and the elements melting, and 'the sea was no more' signals sweeping discontinuity; on this view the emphasis falls less on renovation of the old and more on a fresh, decisive act of creation.

debates.bearing: Either way the outcome is the same: a real, physical, everlasting home where God dwells fully with His redeemed people. The debate concerns the mechanism of transition, not the goodness or reality of the promise.

study.originalLanguages: The vision turns on four words. Kainos names newness of kind, not merely of time, God's declaration 'Behold, I make all things new' reaches to the very quality of creation, not just its age. God will skenoo, tabernacle, with His people, the tent imagery of the wilderness becoming an unveiled, permanent dwelling. He takes the title Alpha kai O, the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, claiming sovereignty over the whole span of history, a title Christ will claim again in 22:13. And the one who nikao, overcomes, inherits all things, the same verb used throughout Revelation 2-3 for persevering faith under pressure.

typology.type: Eden's tree of life barred by the flaming sword, Genesis 3:24
typology.fulfillment: The distance between God and man that began at the exile from Eden begins to close here, as God's dwelling is declared to be with man directly; the tree of life itself is shown freely accessible in the vision that follows, Revelation 22:1-2.

typology.type: The tabernacle and temple where God dwelt among Israel behind a veil, Exodus 25:8
typology.fulfillment: God now dwells with His people without distance or veil, Revelation 21:3, the goal toward which the tabernacle and temple always pointed.

typology.type: Israel and the church betrothed to the LORD, Hosea 2:19-20, Ephesians 5:25-27
typology.fulfillment: The holy city descends as a bride adorned for her husband, the marriage covenant between God and His people brought to its consummation.

study.christ: The One seated on the throne who declares, 'Behold, I make all things new,' names Himself the Alpha and the Omega, the very title the risen Christ claims for Himself in Revelation 1:8, 1:17, and 22:13. His words, 'It is done,' echo His cry from the cross, 'It is finished,' tetelestai: the same finished work that secured salvation now secures new creation. He is the spring who gives freely to the thirsty, and it is by faith in Him, not by achievement, that the one who overcomes becomes His own son.
study.god: God is the One who at last dwells with His people without distance, the ancient tabernacle and temple promise, Leviticus 26:11-12 and Ezekiel 37:27, fulfilled without veil. He is faithful and true to His own words, sovereign to declare 'Behold, I make all things new,' and just, drawing a firm line between those who overcome and those whose unrepentant sin excludes them from His presence.

crossReferences.note: The prophetic root of 'a new heaven and a new earth,' where weeping and crying will no longer be heard.
The LORD will swallow up death forever and wipe away every tear, quoted almost verbatim in Revelation 21:4.
God's everlasting covenant of peace and His dwelling place set among His people forever, fulfilled unveiled here.
The exile from Eden and the flaming sword guarding the tree of life, the ancient distance this new dwelling of God with man begins to close.
Christ again takes the title Alpha and Omega, tying this vision's opening declaration to the book's closing one.

study.oneStory: Genesis 3 ended with humanity driven from Eden and a flaming sword guarding the way back to the tree of life. Revelation 21 begins the answer: God Himself comes to dwell with His people forever, wiping away the very tears that began in the garden, and declaring that the ancient sentence of death is being undone as He makes all things new.

sermon.title: The New Heaven and New Earth
sermon.why: Preached on Revelation 21:1-7 in the series Bible: The Whole Story, Keller shows how the resurrection of Christ is the first installment of the new heaven and new earth, giving suffering believers a living hope rather than an abstract idea.

quiz.q: What does John see pass away in Revelation 21:1?

options.text: The first heaven and the first earth, along with the sea, replaced by a new heaven and a new earth.
options.note: This is the plain statement of verse 1.

options.text: Only the sea, while heaven and earth continue unchanged.
options.note: The sea's passing is notable, but the first heaven and earth themselves are also said to have passed away.

options.text: Only sinful people, while creation itself stays exactly the same.
options.note: The text describes a change in creation itself, not merely in people.

options.text: Nothing; the vision describes no real change at all.
options.note: The whole vision turns on decisive change, a new heaven and a new earth.

quiz.q: What does the loud voice from the throne declare in verse 3?

options.text: That the dwelling place of God is now with man, and He will be their God and they His people.
options.note: This is the Immanuel promise stated directly.

options.text: That the temple in Jerusalem would soon be rebuilt.
options.note: The vision actually moves away from a localized temple; see Revelation 21:22.

options.text: That God would visit His people occasionally from now on.
options.note: The promise is continual, unbroken dwelling, not occasional visits.

options.text: That God remains distant from His people forever.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the voice declares.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by calling Himself the Alpha and the Omega in verse 6?

options.text: That He is the beginning and end of all things, eternal and sovereign over the whole of history.
options.note: Alpha and Omega are the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, a title of eternity.

options.text: That He is merely the first among many gods.
options.note: This misreads the monotheistic force of the title within Revelation's vision of the one true God.

options.text: That He created only the beginning of the world, not its end.
options.note: The title claims both the beginning and the end, not one without the other.

options.text: That the title belongs to an angel rather than to God or Christ.
options.note: The title is claimed by the One on the throne, and by Christ Himself in Revelation 22:13.

quiz.q: What does it mean to 'overcome' in verse 7?

options.text: To hold fast to Christ in persevering faith rather than falling into the sins listed in verse 8.
options.note: This matches the repeated call to overcome addressed to the churches in Revelation 2-3.

options.text: To achieve moral perfection entirely through personal effort.
options.note: Overcoming is persevering faith in Christ, who gives freely from the spring of life, not bare self-effort.

options.text: To defeat other people in worldly conflict.
options.note: This is not the sense of the word in Revelation's letters to the churches.

options.text: To avoid ever facing any hardship in this life.
options.note: Revelation is written precisely to strengthen believers facing hardship, not to promise its absence.

quiz.q: What is the 'second death' named in verse 8?

options.text: Final, eternal exclusion from God's presence in the lake of fire, distinct from ordinary bodily death.
options.note: Revelation names this same second death in 2:11 and 20:6, 14.

options.text: Simply another term for ordinary physical death.
options.note: Revelation distinguishes the second death as a further, final judgment beyond bodily death.

options.text: A temporary punishment after which everyone is restored.
options.note: This softens the stark, final warning language of the passage.

options.text: A blessing promised to those who overcome.
options.note: This is the opposite; the second death is the warning given to the unrepentant, not a reward.

questions: God's own dwelling with His people is the goal of the whole Bible, from Eden to here. Where do you most need to know that God is near, not distant, right now?
Verse 4 promises no more tears, mourning, crying, or pain, the very griefs Genesis 3 introduced into the world. What sorrow are you carrying that this promise speaks directly to?
The Alpha and Omega offers the water of life freely to the thirsty. What are you tempted to work for or earn instead of simply receiving it as a gift?
Overcomers inherit all things by continuing to trust Christ, not by sinless perfection. Where do you need courage to keep trusting rather than shrink back this week?
The list in verse 8 is sobering. Are there patterns there, even in small ways, that God may be calling you to repent of and leave behind?

translationNotes.note: New, kainos, describes newness in kind or quality, not merely in time, neos; much of the debate over renewal versus replacement of creation turns on this distinction.
The Greek verb skenoo, to dwell or tabernacle, recalls the tabernacle where God dwelt among Israel behind a veil, Exodus 25:8; here the dwelling is direct and unmediated.
Alpha and Omega are the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, a title of eternity and sovereignty that Christ takes again for Himself in Revelation 22:13.
The 'second death' is a phrase unique to Revelation, see also 2:11 and 20:6, 14, naming a final judgment beyond ordinary bodily death.

meta.notes: BSB text supplied and pre-verified this session; no em or en dashes were present in the supplied text and none were introduced. Poetic line breaks were already flattened into flowing prose as supplied. Straight quotation marks were converted to curly typographic marks, and the continuous divine speech running from verse 3 into verse 4, and from verse 6 into verse 8, was punctuated as a single closed quotation to match standard BSB rendering; no wording was altered.
